text.title: Hierarchical Manager-Worker Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for shipping a manager-worker hierarchical runner that scales beyond a toy demo.

rule.statement: The manager MUST emit the plan as JSON conforming to `plan-schema.json` (assignments[] of `{id, worker_name, subtask, deps[]}`). If parsing fails, the orchestrator MUST feed the parse error back to the manager for one repair attempt before aborting.
rule.rationale: Malformed plans cause silent skipped workers; one structured repair loop catches ~95% of LLM JSON glitches without unbounded retries.

rule.statement: Before dispatch, the orchestrator MUST run a DAG cycle check on the `deps[]` edges. If the plan contains a cycle, refuse to start and return the cycle to the manager for re-planning.
rule.rationale: Cycle detection is O(V+E) and free of false positives; without it, the run hangs at dispatch time.

rule.statement: The manager MUST NOT perform domain work itself. Its system prompt MUST say "Decompose tasks and coordinate workers"; verbs like "implement", "write code", "research" belong to workers.
rule.rationale: If the manager does domain work, it becomes the very bottleneck this pattern is supposed to remove.

rule.statement: Workers MUST be stateless across subtask invocations. Workers MUST NOT communicate with each other directly — the orchestrator is the only routing surface.
rule.rationale: Stateless workers + single-routing-surface make the trace deterministic and replayable; direct worker-to-worker channels make debugging combinatorial.

rule.statement: The runner MUST declare a partial-failure policy upfront (`abort` / `degrade` / `retry`) AND a per-worker timeout. If a worker times out or errors, the policy decides whether the run aborts, continues without that worker's contribution, or retries N≤2 times.
rule.rationale: Without declared policy, partial failures get swept under "best-effort" semantics that hide bugs and surprise users with degraded outputs.
